sources.title: Roles — Military Occupational Specialty
sources.note: Confirmed reachable (200, ~234KB) but is a general 'roles' landing page organized around a handful of broad career-field video features (e.g. Reconnaissance, Aircraft Maintenance), not a full 4-digit MOS code listing. Not usable as the primary enumeration source for this catalog; used only to confirm marines.com's own OccFld framing.

sources.title: List of United States Marine Corps MOS — Wikipedia
sources.note: Primary enumeration source for this catalog. Organized by OccFld (01-90), lists 4-digit codes, titles, and enlisted/officer rank ranges, with deprecated/deleted/consolidated MOSs marked (strikethrough or explicit 'deleted'/'redesignated' text) and excluded from this catalog. Cross-checked in a targeted sample against the primary-source PDF below with a strong match rate; a handful of same-code title mismatches were found and are documented in 'unverified' below.

sources.title: NAVMC 1200.1D — Military Occupational Specialty Manual (May 2018), hosted at mosmanual.com
sources.note: Official Marine Corps MOS Manual (807 pages), the primary source underlying the Wikipedia list. Successfully fetched and parsed (direct marines.mil / hqmc.marines.mil / usmcu.edu / iandl.marines.mil mirrors all returned HTTP 403). Used to source the 'what_it_is' one-sentence description, ASVAB composite minimum (GT/EL/MM only), and clearance level for 319 of 455 catalog entries via each MOS's official 'Summary' and 'Prerequisites' text. This is a May-2018 snapshot — it predates OccFld 17's 2019-2020 rename from 'Cyberspace Operations' to 'Information Maneuver' and various codes added/renamed 2019-2024, so the remaining 136 entries (added or renamed after 2018) are sourced from the current Wikipedia title only, with a plain factual one-sentence description written from the title's own wording and NOT independently verified against a primary document this session.

unverified: 136 of 455 entries (marked in each entry's own 'source' field as 'title-level only') have no matching entry in the 2018 NAVMC 1200.1D baseline manual, meaning they were added, renamed, or restructured between 2018 and the current (2026) Wikipedia snapshot. Their 'what_it_is' sentences are plain, literal restatements of the official title/equipment (e.g. 'maintains the C-9 aircraft'), written without inventing specifics, but are not cross-checked against a primary post-2018 .mil document this session — no fetchable current-year MOS Manual mirror was found (marines.mil, hqmc.marines.mil, usmcu.edu, and iandl.marines.mil PDF mirrors all returned HTTP 403).

Wikipedia's current 'List of United States Marine Corps MOS' article is itself a secondary/tertiary compilation (its own edit history cites the MOS Manual as its source) and carries an active Wikipedia cleanup banner ('needs reorganization', 'needs rewrite') as of the retrieval date — treat exact current-year code assignments as good-confidence but not primary-source-certain.

During PDF-to-Wikipedia cross-checking, 3 codes were found to have been reused for a materially different job between the 2018 manual and the current list (same 4-digit code, unrelated title/duties): 3451 (2018: 'Financial Management Resource Analyst (FMRA)' -> current: 'Fiscal/Budget Technician'), 5567 (2018: 'Musician, Vocalist' -> current: 'Arranger, Band'), and 8231 (2018: 'Foreign Area Staff Non-Commissioned Officer-Latin America' -> current: 'Education Assistant'). For these 3 the 2018 PDF summary/scores were deliberately discarded and a plain title-based description was written instead, to avoid attaching the wrong job's description to the current code.

ASVAB composite minimums (GT/EL/MM) are populated for 148 of 455 entries, taken directly from the 'Prerequisites' section of the 2018 NAVMC 1200.1D for that specific code. The remaining 307 entries have no minimum listed in the source text within this session's extraction and are left null rather than guessed, per the binding no-fabrication constraint — this does not mean no minimum exists, only that none was found sourced.

Security clearance level is populated (Secret / Top Secret / Top Secret/SCI) for only 19 of 455 entries, based on an explicit clearance-related phrase found within roughly the first 2,500 characters of that MOS's 2018 PDF entry. Many other entries (especially across Intelligence, SIGINT, and Cyberspace OccFlds) likely also require a clearance in practice; null here means 'not found in the scanned text this session,' not 'no clearance required.'

OccFld 27 (Linguist) is deliberately excluded from this catalog as a set of individual entries. The current Wikipedia list shows this field as ~65 individual language-designator codes (e.g. 2737 Chinese (Mandarin), 2768 Spanish) that are Exception-MOS (EMOS) language skill identifiers attached to a Marine's primary MOS (e.g. 0211 CI/HUMINT Specialist), not standalone accession jobs — and the one MOS that historically served as the standalone 'Military Interpreter/Translator' billet (2799) was deleted in 2016 with no confirmed current replacement PMOS found this session. Rather than fabricate a representative code or pad the catalog with ~65 near-duplicate language rows, this OccFld is flagged here as a documented gap.

OccFld 46 (Combat Camera / COMCAM) produced zero catalog entries: every code found in the current Wikipedia list for this field is explicitly tagged NMOS/FMOS (a secondary skill designator on top of another primary MOS, e.g. a 4300-series or 0300-series Marine who is also COMCAM-qualified), not a standalone primary MOS, and this catalog only includes primary (PMOS) jobs.

OccFld 82, 86, 88 (Miscellaneous Requirement MOS) and 97 (Reporting MOS) exist as separate numbered fields in the 2018 primary manual but were not present as separate sections in the current Wikipedia list; their content (regional affairs/political-military billet designators, and DoD reporting-only codes with no actual unit billet) appears to be folded into the current '80 Miscellaneous' and (excluded) '90 Reporting' groupings respectively. Not independently re-verified against the primary source under the current numbering this session.

This catalog excludes: entry-level placeholder 'Basic' MOSs (e.g. 0300 Basic Infantry Marine, awarded before school training and not a distinct job); training-pipeline precursor stages ('-Trainee', 'FRS Basic', 'Student' suffixed codes that are a temporary status en route to a fully qualified code already captured separately); and all non-primary NMOS/FMOS/EMOS/AMOS designators (secondary skill add-ons layered on top of an existing primary MOS, per the Manual's own 'Types of MOSs' definitions). These exclusions are a scoping decision, documented here rather than silently applied, and mean the catalog is smaller than a literal count of every 4-digit code appearing anywhere in the source manual (which running near 900-1,000 if every NMOS/FMOS/EMOS/language-designator/reporting variant were counted individually).
completion_accountability: 455 current, primary (PMOS)-track Marine Corps enlisted and officer occupational specialties cataloged across 39 of the ~44 current OccFlds (01-80; OccFld 27 Linguist and 46 Combat Camera intentionally produced 0 standalone-job entries, documented in 'unverified' above, not silently dropped). Enumeration source: Wikipedia's 'List of United States Marine Corps MOS,' itself organized by OccFld exactly as the task instructed, filtered to exclude deprecated/deleted/consolidated codes, entry-level 'Basic' placeholder codes, training-pipeline precursor codes, and non-primary NMOS/FMOS/EMOS designators. 319 of 455 entries (70%) were cross-verified against the official NAVMC 1200.1D (2018) MOS Manual PDF, sourcing their one-sentence description, and where stated in the source text, an ASVAB GT/EL/MM composite minimum and/or clearance level, directly from the primary document. The remaining 136 entries (30%), added or renamed after the 2018 baseline, are described from their current title alone, flagged as such in each entry's 'source' field, with no invented scores, clearance, pay, or training-length figures. 3 code-reuse collisions between the 2018 manual and the current list were caught and corrected rather than silently mismatched (documented in 'unverified'). Zero fabricated 4-digit codes and zero guessed URLs — every code traces to the Wikipedia enumeration, and the 2018 PDF was fetched directly (mosmanual.com mirror; all *.marines.mil / *.mil mirrors returned HTTP 403). Session tool budget: ~14 fetch/search calls used against the ~25 budget (marines.com roles page, 2 Wikipedia article fetches, 4 failed .mil PDF mirror attempts, 1 web search, 4 successful alternate-mirror PDF attempts, 1 successful 807-page PDF download), leaving headroom that was intentionally redirected into local extraction/cross-verification scripting rather than additional network calls, since the two sources already fetched were sufficient for completeness.
